query: How does display:flex work in CSS?

competitors.label: Basic concepts of flexbox
competitors.selectionReason: This is MDN's canonical flexbox concepts guide for axes, flex items, wrapping, alignment, and initial flex behavior.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on flexbox concepts, default item behavior, and the relationship between axes, alignment, and wrapping.
theirGaps: The guide is broader and less focused on the specific production bugs caused by misunderstanding direct-child scope, shrinking, or one-dimensional limits.

realWorldUseCases: Most flexbox debugging problems come from misunderstanding those rules, not from flex itself being unpredictable.
followUpConfusion:
Common mistakes
  • Only direct children participate. Grandchildren do not become flex items unless their own parent is also a flex container.
  • Flexbox is one-dimensional. It is great for rows or columns, but if you need to control both rows and columns together, grid is usually a better fit.
  • Wrapping is not automatic. Items stay on one line until you add flex-wrap: wrap.
  • Alignment and sizing are separate concerns. justify-content and align-items place items; flex decides how they grow and shrink.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the layout model clearly, but it is less compressed around one production debugging story such as overflow, wrong wrapping, or nested children not becoming flex items.
actionableExamples: The guide includes strong examples for axes, wrapping, and item behavior, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: MDN covers the model thoroughly, but our page is more explicit about the exact next questions interviewers ask: direct children only, flex is one-dimensional, and wrapping is opt-in.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter overflow example showing a child that needs min-width: 0 before shrink behavior makes sense.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical flexbox guide, but our page is more focused on the production and interview follow-ups that explain why flex layouts feel 'broken' in real UI code.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's CSS interview bank is directly relevant and explicitly includes flexbox as a core CSS interview topic.
theirStrengths: It is a strong CSS interview-prep hub with flexbox, grid, box model, and responsive design coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is broad rather than a dedicated display:flex answer, so it is less focused on concrete flex debugging traps.

realWorldUseCases:
Parent vs. children

The important detail is that flexbox is a parent-child layout model. Setting the parent to flex does not automatically affect every nested descendant.
followUpConfusion: Only the direct children become flex items.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd clearly covers flexbox as an interview topic, but the hub page is less explicit about nested descendants, wrap defaults, and shrink-versus-alignment confusion.
actionableExamples: Because the bank provides quick answers and detailed explanations, the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: The landing page is less specific about direct-child scope and about the one-dimensional limit that often triggers the follow-up 'should this be grid instead?'

actionableExamples: Keep one smaller before-versus-after nested layout snippet where only the parent becomes a flex container.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is a useful CSS interview hub, but our page is more topic-specific about the exact flexbox traps that cause production bugs and interview follow-ups.

competitors.label: A Complete Guide to Flexbox
competitors.selectionReason: CSS-Tricks has the most widely referenced flexbox guide and is the strongest tutorial comparator for display:flex fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a very strong property-by-property flexbox reference with dense visual examples.
theirGaps: It is more catalog-like and less focused on one interview-style mental model for why layouts fail in production.

followUpConfusion: Alignment and sizing are separate concerns. justify-content and align-items place items; flex decides how they grow and shrink.

realWorldUseCases: CSS-Tricks is strong on flexbox behavior and syntax, but it is less centered on one production debugging story like nested descendants, overflow, and grid-versus-flex choice.
actionableExamples: Its visual examples and property reference are strong, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The guide is less explicit about the interview-ready distinction between alignment, sizing, wrapping, and one-dimensional limits.

actionableExamples: Add one direct child that refuses to shrink until min-width is adjusted, not just the general parent-child explanation.

competitors.winReason: CSS-Tricks is the stronger property reference, but our page is more reusable as a concise answer because it turns flexbox into a production debugging mental model instead of a syntax catalog.
